coredata.dc:title: Multichannel fluorescent sensor array for discrimination of thiols using carbon dot–metal ion pairs
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shuai
Xu, Chun-Hua
Yu, Yong-Liang
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Thiols play an essential role in mediating physiological processes and maintaining balance in biological systems. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a convenient method for detection and discrimination of thiols. In this work, a facile multichannel fluorescent sensor array was fabricated to detect and discriminate thiols based on carbon dot (CD)–metal ion pairs. The selected metal ions induced aggregation of the CD, and thus quenched their fluorescence. Different thiols exhibited different affinities for the metal ions, resulting in disaggregation or further aggregation of the CD. As a result, the fluorescence of the CD was recovered or further quenched by the addition of thiols, generating distinct fluorescence response patterns. The characteristic response patterns of seven kinds of thiols were differentiated by linear discriminant analysis at a low concentration of 1.0 μM. Moreover, the sensor array was validated by 100% accurate identification of 42 thiols in double-blind experiments, as well as in fetal bovine serum and urine. The present multichannel sensor array possesses a simple fabrication process, and may also be extended to detect and discriminate other analytes with similar chemical structures or properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiols discrimination
Carbon dot
Metal ion
Sensor array
Double-blind experiment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CD by metal ions
Principle and fabrication of the fluorescent sensor array
Response of CD–metal ion pairs to thiols and response mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21605014 and 21475018 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N151003001 and N160504010 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the design and fabrication process of the fluorescent sensor array for the detection and discrimination of thiols.

(A) TEM images of the CD (Inset: size distribution of free CD); (B) AFM images of the CD; (C) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and fluorescence emission spectra of an aqueous dispersion of the CD (Inset: photographs of an aqueous dispersion of CD under daylight (left) and UV light (right)); (D) Fluorescence spectra of the CD with excitation wavelengths from 300 to 360 nm.
Fingerprints of seven thiols (1 μM) based on the fluorescence response patterns of the five-channel CD–metal ion pair sensor array.
AFM images of the CD on the addition of Fe3+ (A) and Ag+ (B); AFM images of the CD–Fe3+ ensemble (C) and CD–Ag+ ensemble (D) on the addition of GSH.

Canonical score plots for the discrimination of the seven thiols at 1.0 μM based on the five-channel CD–metal ion pair sensor (A), the three-channel CD–metal ion pair sensor array of CD–Cu2+, CD–Ag+, and CD–Fe3+ (B), the two-channel CD–metal ion pair sensor array of CD–Cu2+ and CD–Ag+ (C), and the two-channel CD–metal ion pair sensor array of CD–Ag+ and CD–Fe3+ (D).

(A) Canonical score plot for the discrimination of different concentrations (1, 3, 5, 8, and 10 μM) of GSH based on the three-channel CD–metal ion pair sensor array; (B) linear relationship for the concentration of GSH from 1 to 10 μM (error bars correspond to six parallel measurements).

Canonical score plot for the discrimination of thiols mixtures (Hcy/NAC = 100/0, 70/30, 50/50, 30/70, and 0/100 with a total thiol concentration of 1.0 μM) using the five-channel CD–metal ion pair sensor array.
Canonical score plot for the discrimination of seven thiols (5 μM) in urine (A) and fetal bovine serum (B) using the five-channel CD–metal ion pair sensor array.

head.ce:title: Multichannel fluorescent sensor array for discrimination of thiols using carbon dot–metal ion pairs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110169, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang, 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiols play an essential role in mediating physiological processes and maintaining balance in biological systems. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a convenient method for detection and discrimination of thiols. In this work, a facile multichannel fluorescent sensor array was fabricated to detect and discriminate thiols based on carbon dot (CD)–metal ion pairs. The selected metal ions induced aggregation of the CD, and thus quenched their fluorescence. Different thiols exhibited different affinities for the metal ions, resulting in disaggregation or further aggregation of the CD. As a result, the fluorescence of the CD was recovered or further quenched by the addition of thiols, generating distinct fluorescence response patterns. The characteristic response patterns of seven kinds of thiols were differentiated by linear discriminant analysis at a low concentration of 1.0 μM. Moreover, the sensor array was validated by 100% accurate identification of 42 thiols in double-blind experiments, as well as in fetal bovine serum and urine. The present multichannel sensor array possesses a simple fabrication process, and may also be extended to detect and discriminate other analytes with similar chemical structures or properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiols play crucial roles in various physiological functions owing to their widespread occurrence in many biologically significant molecules. Abnormal concentrations of thiols can cause aberrant physiological functions, affecting the normal metabolism of the body and leading to disease [1–3]. For instance, abnormal blood homocysteine (Hcy) concentrations are a high risk for Alzheimer's disease, neural tube defects, osteoporosis, and inflammatory bowel disease [4]. Cysteine (Cys) deficiency may lead to health problems such as gray hair, weight loss, growth failure, and liver damage [5]. Glutathione (GSH) serves as the key regulator for the redox state of protein thiols, and maintains intracellular redox activity, xenobiotic metabolism, intracellular signal transduction, and gene regulation [6,7]. Therefore, the detection and discrimination of different thiols is important for our understanding of problems in living systems.

At present, the methods for analyzing and detecting thiols include high-performance liquid chromatography [8], mass spectrometry [9], and capillary electrophoresis [10]. However, these methods cannot be used for extensive detection of thiols owing to their high costs and cumbersome operation. Sensing techniques have received much attention owing to the benefits of excellent sensitivity, favorable selectivity, operational simplicity, real-time detection, and portability. Numerous sensors have already been developed for thiol detection, including GSH, Cys, and Hcy, but they exhibit a lack of specificity owing to the high similarity between different types of thiols.

Array sensors may provide a solution to this problem. Recently, sensing techniques have been split into two categories: [11] (1) The first category is highly specific sensing using an ideal sensor that has complete specificity for a single analyte with no response to others. However, this specificity is rarely achievable owing to the high similarity between analytes or a lack of tools for specific sensing of the target. (2) The second category is selective sensing using an array, which is often the best that can be achieved and is quite useful. Arrays have many advantages over individual sensor elements, including accuracy, diversity, and the capacity for simultaneous detection and discrimination of a series of analytes with similar structures or properties. Sensor arrays based on cross-responsive selective sensing elements have been developed to detect and discriminate diverse analytes, e.g., proteins [12–14], explosives [15], and metal ions [16,17]. Although these sensor arrays with high selectivity have been developed, improvements in sensitivity are still required [18–20]. Furthermore, very few sensor arrays have developed for the discrimination of thiols. Recently, benefiting from the high efficiency of enzyme-catalyzed reactions, a colorimetric sensor array with improved sensitivity was developed, and six kinds of thiols were successfully discriminated at a relatively low concentration of 1.0 μM [21]. However, such bio-enzyme reactions are high-cost, and may result in unsatisfactory stability and repeatability during massive test owing to their vulnerable activities, e.g., very sensitive to pH, temperature, chemical reagents, etc. Therefore, it is still highly desirable to construct a simple sensor array for detection and discrimination of thiols with both high selectivity and sensitivity.

In this work, a multichannel fluorescent sensor array based on carbon dot (CD)–metal ion pairs was fabricated to identify various thiols. CD can interact with metal ions because of the abundant functional groups covering their surfaces [22]. Metal ions were selected to assemble with the CD, resulting in the quenching of the CD fluorescence through aggregation. On the addition of thiols, different fluorescence response signals were obtained owing to the different affinities of the thiols for the various metal ions. According to the distinct changes in fluorescence of the CD, the thiols were identified and differentiated by linear discriminant analysis (LDA). The sensor array not only allowed discrimination of thiols at concentrations as low as 1.0 μM, but also realized simultaneous discrimination of a mixture of two thiols at the same concentration and at different molar ratios. In addition, quantitative detection of GSH was achieved using this array, and its potential for identifying thiols in real and double-blind samples was also demonstrated.

Citric acid, sodium hydroxide, nitric acid, chromium(III) chloride, copper(II) chloride, iron(III) chloride, silver nitrate, lead(II) nitrate, cysteine (Cys), 2-mercaptoethanol (MCE), glutathione (GSH), N-acetylcysteine (NAC), homocysteine (Hcy), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), and mercaptoacetic acid (MAA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). 5, 5′-dithio-bis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water of 18 MΩ cm was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired on a Dimension Icon atomic force microscope (Bruker, Germany) using tapping mode with a ScanAsyst Air tip. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (H-7650, Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet-6700 spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron, USA) between 400 and 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo Electron, USA) with an Al-K excitation source (280 eV). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using an X’Pert Pro MPDDY2094 diffractometer (Panalytical B.V, Netherlands) with Cu-Kα irradiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The surface charge properties of the CD in aqueous solution at pH 2–11 were measured using a ZEN3600 Nano Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) with a 1.0 cm optical path. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). Fluorescence quenching and recovery experiments were performed using a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader (BioTek, USA).

The CD were synthesized by a microwave hydrothermal method. 1.05 g of citric acid and 1.21 g of Cys were dissolved in 10 mL of DI water. Then, the mixture was sealed into a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in a microwave at 180 °C for 10 min. After cooling to room temperature, an orange-brown solution was obtained. The solution was dialyzed against DI water with a dialysis bag (MWCO membrane, 100–500 Da) for 24 h to remove excess precursors. The purified products were freeze-dried to obtain a uniform powder for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CD by metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: First, 400 μL of 7 μg mL−1 CD at pH 5.0 and 400 μL of metal ions (Ag+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, or Cr3+) at various concentrations within 0–20 mM in HNO3 solution at pH 5.0 were added sequentially into a 2-mL tube. Then, after incubating at room temperature for 30 min, 200 μL of the mixed solution was transferred into a 96-well plate. The fluorescence intensity of each sample was recorded at λex/λem = 350/420 nm using a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader.

The quenching process was first performed as described above: 400 μL of 7 μg mL−1 CD in HNO3 solution at pH 5.0 and 400 μL of metal ions (Ag+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, or Pb2+) at the optimized concentration in HNO3 solution at pH 5.0 were incubated in a 2-mL tube for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity variation ((I − I0)/I0) of the working solutions at λex/λem = 350/420 nm was recorded in the absence and presence of 400 μL of analyte (Cys, MCE, GSH, NAC, Hcy, MPA, or MAA) on a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader after incubation for 60 min. Finally, the obtained data were processed using LDA in SPSS v17.0.

To identify unknown samples, 300 μL of Cys with different concentrations (5, 15, 25, 35, and 50 μM) was added to the mixture solution of 200 μL of DTNB (2 mM) and 100 μL of PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). After incubation for 5 min, the absorbance of the solution was measured. Then, a calibration curve for sulfhydryl group was obtained using Cys as the standard. For the 42 unknown samples, 300 μL of each sample was added to the mixture solution of 200 μL of DTNB (2 mM) and 100 μL of PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). After incubation for 5 min, the absorbance of the solution was measured, and the concentration of sulfhydryl group was calculated through the calibration curve.
After determining the concentration, all the unknown samples were diluted to 1 μM. The analysis process was similar to the sensing procedure, as described in Section 2.5.

Thiol-containing analytes in biological samples, i.e., 1% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 10% (v/v) urine, were used as examples. The analysis process was similar to the sensing procedure, as described in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and fabrication of the fluorescent sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions have been reported to quench the fluorescence of some CD by triggering aggregation owing to coordination interactions between the metal ions and the functional groups on the CD (e.g., amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups) [22]. On the other hand, sulfhydryl groups (SH) are known to exhibit high affinities for metal ions [18,21]. Therefore, the presence of thiols will induce changes in the formation of CD–metal ion aggregates, and will cause variation in their fluorescence signals. Furthermore, other functional groups in thiol-containing molecules, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, may give rise to differences in affinities. For each target analyte, the array of CD–metal ion pairs may render a unique response pattern, which can be further differentiated via LDA. In this work, a multichannel fluorescent sensor array was fabricated by using five kinds of CD–metal ion pairs based on the competing interactions of metal ions (i.e., Ag+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, and Pb2+) with CD and thiols. The principle of the as-fabricated array is shown in Scheme 1 . To verify the discrimination ability of the present sensor array, seven kinds of thiols (i.e., Cys, MCE, GSH, NAC, Hcy, MPA, and MAA) with different structures and functionalities were chosen as analytes. The basic chemical properties of the seven thiols used in the sensing experiments are listed in Table S1.

The CD used to construct the sensor array were prepared using a microwave hydrothermal method with citric acid and Cys as precursors. The as-prepared CD were characterized thoroughly to confirm their morphology, composition, and physical properties (Fig. 1 and Figs. S1–S4). The TEM and AFM images in Fig. 1A, B demonstrate that the CD are well dispersed with an average diameter of ∼16 nm and an average height of 2–3 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. S3) shows a broad diffraction peak at 23°, revealing an amorphous carbon phase [23]. The surface functional groups of the CD were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS, as shown in Fig. S1 and S2 . In the FT-IR spectrum of the CD, NH stretching vibrations and the vibrational absorption bands of CO, COC, and OH were observed. The XPS data in Fig. S2 confirm that carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amide groups decorate the surface of the CD [24–26]. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CD are shown in Fig. 1C. A characteristic absorption peak is observed at 345 nm, which corresponds to the n–π* electron transition of carbonyl groups (CO) [24]. The maximum emission intensity was centered at 420 nm with an excitation wavelength of 345 nm. The quantum yield of the CD was 7.1%. As shown in Fig. 1D, the obtained CD show classic excitation-independent PL behavior, as the emission wavelength of the CD does not change when the excitation wavelength is varied from 300 to 360 nm. As shown in Fig. S4A, as the pH increases from 2.0 to 11.0, the zeta potential of the CD decreases gradually from −1.86 to −25.4 mV. Additionally, the decrease of pH within 5.0–2.0 causes a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CD. This observation indicates that the protonation states of the surface functional groups, including carbonyl, carboxyl, and amide groups, change [27]. These functional groups allow the as-prepared CD to chelate with various metal ions. Consequently, the as-prepared CD meet the requirements for use in the sensor array design.

As the sensor array is based on the competing interactions of metal ions with CD and analytes, the appropriate concentrations of metal ions and CD are critical for the formation of CD–metal ion ensembles. Therefore, titration experiments were carried out to determine the fluorescence response of CD (pH = 5.0) to Ag+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, and Pb2+. As shown in Fig. S5A, the slope of the curves for Cr3+, Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+, and Pb2+ were steepest within the concentration range of 0–20 mM, indicating that the quenching efficiency of the metal ions for CD fluorescence is the best in this concentration range. Taking into account the binding ability and the sensitivity of the analyte to the metal ions, 0.5 mM Cr3+, 1.0 mM Cu2+, 1.0 mM Ag+, 0.5 mM Fe3+, and 1.0 mM Pb2+ were selected to construct the sensor array. To achieve the best sensing performance, the concentration of CD was also optimized. As shown in Fig. S5B, the CD fluorescence decreased when Cr3+ was added to the solution, and the most sensitive fluorescence response was observed for 7 μg mL−1 CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CD–metal ion pairs to thiols and response mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: First, the effects of the thiols on the fluorescence intensity of the CD were studied. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of the CD varies slightly in the presence of thiols, indicating that these analytes hardly have a significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of CD at a high concentration of 300 μM. Then, the thiols at a concentration of 1.0 μM were added to solutions of the five kinds of CD–metal ion pair. The relative fluorescence variation, i.e., (I − I0)/I0, induced by each thiol against the sensor array were recorded to construct a specific fingerprint map (Fig. 2 ). The fingerprints contain relevant information about the CD–metal ion sensor array response to these thiols. For example, after the addition of thiols, the fluorescence of CD–Fe3+ recovered, whereas the fluorescence of CD–Ag+ was further quenched.

The fluorescence intensity of CD itself is really stable within 2 h (Fig. S4B), and particle size of CD remains virtually unchanged before and after adjusting pH value (Fig. S4C, D). To identify the response mechanism of the sensor array, AFM images of the CD–metal ion ensembles were obtained in the absence and presence of the analytes, using CD–Fe3+ and CD–Ag+ in the presence and absence of GSH as examples. It is obvious that the CD are well dispersed in the absence of metal ions (Fig. 1A, B). However, the addition of metal ions (i.e., Fe3+ and Ag+) triggers aggregation of the CD to various extents, as shown in Fig. 3 A, B and Fig. S7A, B, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Subsequently, the effect of adding GSH on the morphology of the CD–metal ion ensembles was investigated. Disaggregation was realized for the CD–Fe3+ ensembles on the addition of GSH, as shown in Fig. 3C and Fig. S7C, which is in good accordance with the observed fluorescence recovery. Fe3+ ions should have a stronger affinity for GSH than for the functional groups on the CD, and thus they are displaced from the CD by adding GSH, leading to disaggregation of the CD and fluorescence recovery. On the contrary, the addition of GSH causes the CD–Ag+ ensembles to form tighter aggregates (Fig. 3D and Fig. S7D), resulting in further quenching of the CD fluorescence. The effect of GSH on the CD–Ag+ ensemble might be caused by the similar affinities of Ag+ for GSH and the functional groups on the CD, which would allow Ag+ and GSH to behave as a linker to connect more CD together[28].

As shown in Fig. 2, the fluorescence response pattern of each thiol is unique, indicating the practicability of discrimination using the CD–metal ion sensor array. Discrimination of the thiols from each other was achieved by means of statistical analysis. For each thiol, the fluorescence responses against the sensor array were tested six times in parallel, generating a matrix of 5 metals × 7 thiols × 6 replicates. To investigate the fluorescence response pattern of the CD–metal ion array against different target thiols, classical LDA was performed to transform the matrix into canonical factors. Factor 1 accounts for 62.1% of the total variability in the data, whereas Factor 2 contributes 20.9% and Factor 3 contributes 14.0%. The first three factors contribute ∼97.0% of the total variability, whereas the first two factors contribute ∼83.0% of the total variability. The first two factors can be visualized in a two-dimensional canonical score plot, as shown in Fig. 4 A. Each thiol at 1.0 μM induces a specific array response, and is clustered into a separated group by the LDA. To further evaluate the discriminative capability of this sensor array, different concentrations of the thiols were examined. As shown in Fig. S8, all of the investigated thiols at 10 μM are separated from each other with no errors or misclassifications.

Considering the good sensing performance of the sensor array with five sensors, we tried to reduce the sensor number. The results of LDA with five probes illustrate that CD–Cu2+, CD–Ag+, and CD–Fe3+ are the highest contributors to the thiols identification (Fig. S9A). Therefore, the first simplified sensor array was constructed using three sensor units, i.e., CD–Cu2+, CD–Ag+, and CD–Fe3+. The canonical score plot, as shown in Fig. 4B, reveals that the new sensor array can also separate the seven kinds of thiols without overlap between the clusters. The results of LDA with three probes indicate that CD–Ag+ has the highest contribution, and the other two sensor units have the similar contribution (Fig. S9B). The sensor array was further simplified, and the new sensor array consisting of CD–Cu2+ and CD–Ag+, also achieves 100% classification accuracy of the thiols (Fig. 4C). As shown in Fig. 4D, the CD–Ag+ and CD–Fe3+ sensor array gives a classification accuracy of 92.9%. These results clearly demonstrate that the as-fabricated sensor array is of high specificity and differentiation capability for thiols. Note that reducing the number of the sensor units has no significant effect on the working performance of the sensor array, and just two sensors are able to achieve the differentiation of seven thiols. Remarkably, Fig. 4A showed that the MCE and MAA groups were tangent, indicating that the limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 1.0 μM. Comparing to the CD-Ag+ array [18], we also utilize the fluorescence response of CD to metal ion and thiols. However, three types of CD are needed to be prepared in their paper, along with more synthetic work than our design. In addition, the LOD of our method is obviously lower than 10 μM of the CD-Ag+ array. It may be attributed to the larger differences among different metal ions in our system than the three carbon dots in the CD-Ag+ array.

For further illustration, the as-developed sensor array was used to discriminate different concentrations of GSH. As shown in Fig. 5 A, good discrimination of GSH at different concentrations is achieved on a two-dimensional LDA plot. In addition, Fig. 5B shows a distinct linear relationship between the first discriminant factor and the concentrations of GSH ranging from 1 to 10 μM. These results are consistent with those obtained for titration of the thiols against CD–Cu2+ CD–Ag+, and CD–Fe3+, which show linear fluorescence responses within the concentration range of 1–10 μM, as shown in Fig. S10.

Although successful discrimination of the thiols was realized at specific concentrations of 1 and 10 μM, it is far more challenging to discriminate the thiols at unknown concentrations or coexisting with other thiols. It is because that different concentrations and different molar ratios of thiols lead to alterations of the response patterns. To further test the as-developed sensor array, double-blind experiments were performed. To simplify the problem, a predetermination method was adopted for the rapid determination of the total concentration of sulfhydryl groups. The frequently used Ellman’s reagent (DTNB) reacts with the sulfhydryl groups of all seven thiols, and gives the same absorbance (Fig. S11A). A calibration curve was established using Cys as a standard for the determination of sulfhydryl groups in unknown thiol samples (Fig. S11B). After the determination of the sulfhydryl group concentration, the unknown sample was diluted to a final solution containing 1 μM sulfhydryl groups, and then analyzed using the as-developed sensor array. As shown in Table S2, all 42 blind samples were correctly identified with no false negatives. Furthermore, mixtures of Hcy and NAC with different molar ratios (Hcy/NAC = 100/0, 70/30, 50/50, 30/70, and 0/100 with a total concentration of 1.0 μM) were used to evaluate the discrimination ability of the sensor array for coexisting thiols. As shown in Fig. 6 , the five samples were distributed clearly into five independent clusters in the canonical score plot, indicating that these five binary mixtures of Hcy and NAC can be differentiated in the LDA plot.

Firstly, we measured the anti-interference capability of the developed array. The common interfering agents including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, and NO3 − were at the concentration of 500 μM, and other amino acids including Valine, Glycine, and Glutamate were at 50 μM. The sensing result illustrated that they are all clearly separated from the thiols (all at 5 μM) as shown in Fig. S12.

Finally, the applicability of the as-developed sensor array was demonstrated by identifying the seven thiols in biological samples. In order to eliminate matrix effects, the thiols were spiked in FBS (1% v/v) and urine (10% v/v). Then, the fluorescence response of the sensor array to each of the seven thiols at 5 μM in the presence of FBS and urine was measured. LDA revealed that the different responses of the analytes provided unique patterns that effectively classified Cys, MCE, GSH, NAC, Hcy, MPA, and MAA into seven distinct groups, as shown in Fig. 7 A, B. Accordingly, these results clearly illustrate that the sensor array has great potential for identifying thiols in the presence of real biological samples.

In summary, a fluorescence sensor array based on CD–metal ion ensembles was developed to detect and discriminate diverse thiols. The sensor array produced a variety of fluorescence response patterns owing to the disaggregation or further aggregation of the CD–metal ion ensembles caused by the addition of thiols. LDA of the distinct fingerprints clustered the patterns into different groups for each target thiol. Using this sensor array, seven thiols were successfully discriminated at a low concentration of 1.0 μM. Notably, the sensor units in the sensor array can be reduced to two without influencing the discrimination performance, indicating the high capability of this approach for thiol analysis. Furthermore, double-blind samples of thiols were identified with 100% accuracy by this sensor array in combination with the Ellman method. Moreover, this sensor array also showed good potential and anti-interference performance in complex media, e.g., FBS and urine. Improved performance, detection of more analytes, and expanded application may be achieved by adjusting the number, type, and composition of the CD–metal ion pair ensembles in the sensor array or the detection conditions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21605014 and 21475018), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N151003001 and N160504010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.03.174.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alizarin red S/copper ion-based ensemble for fluorescence turn on detection of glutathione with tunable dynamic range
Critical roles of intracellular thiols and calcium in parthenolide-induced apoptosis in human colorectal cancer cells
Expanding the functional diversity of proteins through cysteine oxidation
Folate and homocysteine metabolisms and their roles in the biochemical basis of neuropsychiatry
Lead phthalocyanine as a selective carrier for preparation of a cysteine-selective electrode
Cyanine-based fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of glutathione in cell cultures and live mouse tissues
Probing oxidative stress: small molecule fluorescent sensors of metal ions, reactive oxygen species, and thiols
Methodology for a rapid and simultaneous determination of total cysteine homocysteine, cysteinylglycine and glutathione in plasma by isocratic RP-HPLC
Determination of thiols by capillary electrophoresis with amperometric detection at a coenzyme pyrroloquinoline quinone modified electrode
Selectivity and specificity: Pros and cons in sensing
Near infrared fluorescent dual ligand functionalized Au NCs based multidimensional sensor array for pattern recognition of multiple proteins and serum discrimination
Suspension array of ionic liquid or ionic liquid–quantum dots conjugates for the discrimination of proteins and bacteria
DNA-catalytically active gold nanoparticle conjugates-based colorimetric multidimensional sensor array for protein discrimination
Multichannel detection and differentiation of explosives with a quantum dot array
Functionalized ionic microgel sensor array for colorimetric detection and discrimination of metal ions
A DNA-based label-free artificial tongue for pattern recognition of metal ions
Carbon nanodots-based fluorescent turn-on sensor array for biothiols
A colorimetric indicator-displacement assay array for selective detection and identification of biological thiols
A colorimetric sensor array for detection and discrimination of biothiols based on aggregation of gold nanoparticles
Colorimetric sensor array for thiols discrimination based on urease?metal ion pairs
Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications
Facile microwave-assisted solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-sulfur-codoped carbon quantum dots for cellular imaging applications
Simultaneously fabrication of free and solidified N, S-doped graphene quantum dots via a facile solvent-free synthesis route for fluorescent detection

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of iron (III) and Escherichia coli
Green preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from silkworm chrysalis for cell imaging
Applying carbon dots-metal ions ensembles as a multichannel fluorescent sensor array: detection and discrimination of phosphate anions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yong-Liang Yu received his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry (2010) from Northeastern University, China. In 2008–2009, he worked with Professor Manuel Miro as a visiting student at University of the Balearic Islands, Spain, by developing analytical schemes for the hyphenation of micro-solid phase extraction to liquid chromatography for automatic multiresidue analysis. In 2010, he joined Department of Chemistry of Northeastern University as a lecturer, and since then he has devoted to sample pretreatment and spectroscopic analysis. He is currently a Professor in Analytical Chemistry, in the Department of Chemistry, Northeastern University. He has published more than 40 papers in peer-reviewed international journals.
